practicals.title: Plants: Structure and Function
practicals.aim: To study the leaf structure, arrangement of leaves and venation
procedure: Collect leaves of different plants found in your surroundings. Wash them with water and wipe them dry.
Select one leaf of each plant and keep it between the folds of a newspaper.
Every day, change the newspaper to avoid fungal growth.
After a week, observe the structure and venation of each leaf.
conclusion: Based on the structure of the leaf blade, there are two types of leaves: simple leaves and compound leaves.
Leaves of different plants differ in the shape of the leaf blade, margin, apex, and the presence or absence of petiole and stipule.
There are two types of venation: reticulate venation and parallel venation.
explanation: Reticulate venation - Veins are interconnected and form a fine web-like structure.
Parallel venation - Veins run parallel to each other.

practicals.title: Observation of Fungus
practicals.aim: To observe fungus on the bread.
procedure: Take a piece of bread or roti on a glass slide. Dampen it with a little water. Keep it in a container.
Place it in a warm and humid corner.
Observe the bread piece after 2–3 days.
The stale bread shows fine cottony growth of fungal hyphae belonging to Mucor.
Take a few fragments of this fungus and observe under the microscope.
observation: Most of the fungal hyphae are horizontally spreading.
There are some erect hyphae called sporangiophores.
At the end of these sporangiophores, there are rounded structures called sporangia.
A single sporangium liberates black coloured spores.
Root-like structures called rhizoids are clearly seen under the microscope.
practicals.conclusion: Mucor is non-green, heterotrophic, and eukaryotic. It is saprotrophic as it grows on decomposing organic matter and belongs to the kingdom Fungi.

practicals.title: Static Electricity
practicals.aim: To study the presence of static electricity
procedure: Keep a straw on a glass bottle. Bring another straw near the first straw and observe.
Rub the second straw on a woollen cloth and bring it near the straw kept on the bottle. Observe.
Rub both straws on the woollen cloth. Keep one on the bottle and bring the other near it.
Bring the woollen cloth (used to rub the straw) near the straw kept on the bottle. Observe.

observationsAndConclusions.conclusion: No force is observed between two uncharged objects
A charged object can attract an uncharged object

practicals.explanation: When certain objects are rubbed together, one becomes positively charged and the other becomes negatively charged.

practicals.title: Preparation of Ferric Sulphide and Study its Properties
practicals.aim: To prepare the compound iron sulphide in the laboratory and study its properties.
procedure: Take two evaporating dishes. Take 7 g iron filings in the first dish and 4 g sulphur powder in the second.
Take a horseshoe magnet near the matter in both the dishes and observe the effect of magnet on it.
Observe the colours of iron filings and sulphur powder.
Transfer the entire iron filings from the first dish to the second. Stir with a glass rod and observe the colour of the mixture and the effect of taking the horseshoe magnet near this mixture.
Now heat the matter in the second dish on a tripod stand till it becomes red hot. Let it cool.
Observe the colour change, if any, in the matter and observe whether there is any effect of the horseshoe magnet on it.
Record all your observations.

observations.beforeHeating: The black colour of iron filings and yellow colour of sulphur are observed distinctly
observations.afterHeating: The characteristic yellow colour of sulphur disappeared after heating the mixture

observations.observation: Effect of horseshoe magnet on the matter
observations.beforeHeating: Iron filings get attracted towards magnet
observations.afterHeating: The matter obtained after heating does not get attracted towards magnet

conclusion: The properties of components of a mixture are retained in the mixture.
The properties of components of a compound get changed in compound state.

practicals.title: Identification of Acids and Bases using Indicators
practicals.aim: To identify acids and bases using indicators.
procedure: Take 5 ml of lime juice in four different test tubes. Keep them in a test tube stand.
Dip red litmus paper strip in the solution in test tube 1 and observe.
Dip blue litmus paper strip in the solution in test tube 2 and observe.
Add 3–4 drops of phenolphthalein in test tube 3 and observe.
Add 3–4 drops of methyl orange in test tube 4 and observe.
Repeat the same steps for vinegar, ammonium hydroxide, baking soda solution, soap solution, dilute hydrochloric acid and distilled water.
conclusion: Indicators help in identifying acids and bases.
Distilled water is neutral in nature.

practicals.title: Identification of Acids and Bases using Indicators
practicals.aim: To identify acids and bases using indicators.
procedure: Repeat the indicator tests for all given solutions.
Observe and record the colour changes carefully.
conclusion: Indicators are reliable tools for identifying acidic and basic substances.

practicals.title: To find the pH of various solutions using (A) pH paper and (B) universal indicator
practicals.aim: To find the pH of the following solutions using (A) pH paper and (B) universal indicator: Dilute HCl, Dilute NaOH solution, Lemon juice, Distilled water, Acetic (ethanoic) acid solution, Sodium sulphate solution, Ammonium chloride solution, Sodium bicarbonate solution.

procedure: Take eight clean and dry test tubes and label them from 1 to 8.
Take 10 ml of each given solution in the respective test tubes.
Place a small piece of pH paper on a white glazed tile.
Using a dropper, add 1–2 drops of the solution from test tube 1 onto the pH paper.
Observe the colour change and compare it with the pH chart.
Note the approximate pH value.
Repeat the same steps for test tubes 2 to 8.
Take eight clean and dry test tubes and label them from 1 to 8.
Add 10 ml of each given solution into the respective test tubes.
Using a dropper, add two drops of universal indicator solution to each test tube.
Observe the colour produced in each test tube.
Compare the colour with the universal indicator colour chart.
Note the approximate pH value for each solution.

conclusion: Solutions with pH less than 7 are acidic.
Solutions with pH more than 7 are basic.
Solutions with pH equal to 7 are neutral.
Universal indicator and pH paper both help in estimating the pH of a solution.

practicals.title: To study the neutralization with the help of acid and base
practicals.aim: To study the neutralization reaction using an acid and a base.
procedure: Wash the burette with water and rinse it with hydrochloric acid. Fill the burette with dilute HCl and remove air bubbles, if any.
Wash the pipette with water and rinse it with sodium hydroxide solution. Using the pipette, transfer 10 ml of NaOH solution into a clean conical flask.
Add 1–2 drops of phenolphthalein indicator to the NaOH solution in the conical flask.
Keep the conical flask on a white tile.
Add hydrochloric acid solution from the burette slowly into the conical flask while swirling continuously.
Observe the change in colour of the solution in the conical flask.
Stop adding acid when the pink colour just disappears.
Record the burette reading.
Repeat the experiment to obtain consistent readings.
conclusion: Hydrochloric acid reacts with sodium hydroxide to form salt and water.
The acidic nature of HCl is neutralized by the basic nature of NaOH.
This reaction is called a neutralization reaction.

practicals.title: Determination of the effective resistance of the parallel combination of two resistors
practicals.aim: To determine the effective resistance of the parallel combination of two resistors.
procedure: Select two resistors and note their resistance values.
Connect the circuit as shown in the diagram with the resistors in parallel.
Connect the ammeter in series and the voltmeter across the parallel combination.
Insert the plug key and adjust the rheostat to obtain a suitable current.
Note the readings of ammeter and voltmeter.
Change the current by adjusting the rheostat and note different readings.
Repeat the experiment for each resistor individually by removing the other resistor.
Disconnect the circuit after completing the observations.
practicals.result: The effective resistance of the given parallel combination of resistors is approximately 16.5 ohms.
conclusion: The effective resistance of resistors connected in parallel is less than the smallest individual resistance.
The reciprocal of the effective resistance is equal to the sum of the reciprocals of individual resistances.

practicals.title: Verification of the laws of refraction of light
practicals.aim: To measure the angle of incidence, angle of refraction and angle of emergence and verify the laws of refraction of light.
procedure: Fix a drawing paper on the drawing board using drawing pins.
Place the rectangular glass slab on it and trace its boundary.
Remove the glass slab and draw a normal at a point on the boundary.
Draw an incident ray making a known angle with the normal.
Fix two pins vertically on the incident ray.
Place the glass slab back in its original position.
Observe the images of the pins through the glass slab and fix two more pins in line with the images.
Remove the pins and glass slab and draw the refracted and emergent rays.
Measure the angles of incidence, refraction and emergence.
Repeat the experiment for different angles of incidence.
Calculate the ratio sin i / sin r for each observation.

observations.rayPath: The path of the ray for angles of incidence 30°, 45° and 60° is OABC.

inference: The incident ray and emergent ray are parallel to each other.
The lateral displacement depends on the thickness of the glass slab, refractive index of glass and angle of incidence.
When light travels from air to glass, it bends towards the normal.
The angle of incidence is equal to the angle of emergence.
The incident ray, refracted ray and the normal lie in the same plane.
conclusion: The ratio of sine of angle of incidence to sine of angle of refraction is constant.
This constant is called the refractive index of the second medium with respect to the first medium.
Thus, the laws of refraction of light are verified.
precautions: Pins should be well separated to avoid error in alignment.
Glass slab should not be disturbed while fixing pins.

practicals.title: Determination of the focal length of a convex lens
practicals.aim: To determine the focal length of a convex lens by obtaining an image of a distant object.
procedure: Mount the convex lens on the lens stand.
Select a distant object and turn one surface of the lens towards the object.
Place the white screen on the screen holder and adjust it in front of the lens.
Move the screen towards or away from the lens to obtain a sharp image.
Measure the distance between the lens and the screen.
Repeat the procedure using two more distant objects.
Record the observations in the observation table.
Rotate the lens by 180° so that the other surface faces the distant object.
Obtain a sharp image and measure the distance again.
Repeat for other distant objects.
Find the average focal length of the lens.
Compare f₁ and f₂ to check whether the lens is symmetric.
inference: The average focal length of the given convex lens is 19.8 cm.
f1 is approximately equal to f2.
precautions: Obtain a sharp image of the distant object on the screen.
